title: Gradient Step Calculation
concept: Calculus uses the derivative (slope) of the cost function to calculate the direction (positive or negative) and magnitude of weight adjustments.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the difference between a local and a global minimum?

options.text: A global minimum is the absolute lowest error point, while a local minimum is just a temporary dip.
A local minimum is always better than a global minimum.
A global minimum is only found in simple models.

exercises.explain: A global minimum is the absolute best solution with the lowest overall error. A local minimum is a deceptive local dip.

cards.title: Knowing which way to turn
cards.body: If you are blindfolded on a hill, how do you know which way is down? You feel the slope. If the ground rises to your left, you step to your right.

cards.title: The Gradient Step
cards.body: A gradient step is the mathematical update where we subtract a fraction of the gradient (slope) from the current weights: New Weight = Old Weight - (Learning Rate * Slope).

cards.title: The sign post
cards.body: The slope's direction acts as a signpost. If the slope is positive (uphill), subtracting it moves us backward (downhill). If the slope is negative (downhill), subtracting it moves us forward.

cards.title: Calculating the shift
cards.body: If our weight is 5, the learning rate is 0.1, and the slope is 10 (steeply uphill), the update is 5 - (0.1 * 10) = 4. The weight decreases to move downhill.

cards.title: No guesswork
cards.body: Gradient Descent doesn't randomly try different weights. It uses the exact mathematical derivative of the cost function to calculate the precise direction of steepest descent.

cards.title: Slope sign updates
cards.body: How the sign of the slope determines weight adjustments:

left.text:
Slope is positive (uphill).
Subtracting it decreases the weight to go left (downhill).

right.text:
Slope is negative (downhill).
Subtracting it increases the weight to go right (downhill).

exercises.prompt: Why do we subtract the gradient (slope) from the weight instead of adding it?

options.text: To move downhill toward lower error, since adding it would move us uphill
Because subtraction is faster for computers to calculate than addition
To keep the weights from becoming negative numbers

exercises.explain: The gradient points uphill. Since we want to go downhill (minimize error), we must subtract the gradient.

exercises.prompt: True or false: If the slope of the cost function is zero, the weight will not change during the update.
exercises.explain: True! When slope is zero, the update term becomes zero, meaning the weights stop changing. The model has converged.

exercises.sentence: The mathematical tool used to calculate the slope of the cost function is the ___.
exercises.explain: In calculus, the derivative measures the rate of change or slope of a function at a specific point.

exercises.prompt: If your current weight is 3.0, the learning rate is 0.2, and the slope (gradient) at this point is -5.0, what is the new weight?
exercises.explain: Subtracting the update: 3.0 - (0.2 * -5.0) = 3.0 - (-1.0) = 4.0. Since the slope was negative, the weight increased to go downhill.

exercises.prompt: Your linear model predicts sales. At the current weight, the slope of the error is 50 (steeply positive/uphill). How will the next gradient step adjust the weight?

options.text: It will decrease the weight because a positive slope means we must move backward to go downhill
It will increase the weight because a positive slope means we should go forward
It will keep the weight the same because the slope is positive

exercises.explain: Excellent reasoning! A positive slope means going forward goes uphill (increases error). So we subtract it to decrease the weight and move downhill. Brilliant job on gradient calculations!
